critic_raw:
A data leakage flaw undermines both the CNN and traditional pipelines. The design explicitly states that pixel normalization statistics (mean, std) are computed from the entire dataset before splitting, and feature scaling for the traditional baseline is similarly applied prior to splitting. This means test-set distributional properties inform normalization applied during training, violating the holdout principle. In dermatoscopic classification where inter-device color calibration differences are a primary source of variability, leaking cross-device normalization statistics into training provides an artifactual advantage unavailable at deployment time.

The data selection strategy introduces survivorship bias that threatens external validity. Restricting training data exclusively to images with inter-rater agreement above 90% optimizes for an unambiguous slice of the distribution. Real clinical deployment encounters the full spectrum of image ambiguity; a model trained only on high-agreement cases may fail precisely on borderline lesions where automated assistance would be most valuable. The description does not report what fraction of the original database was excluded by this filter, nor does it characterize how excluded images differ from those retained in terms of lesion type or image quality.

The experiment lacks a clinically grounded baseline. Logistic regression on handcrafted features is a reasonable methodological foil, but the relevant clinical comparator for a dermatoscopic system is expert dermatologist performance on the same images or a validated scoring rule (e.g., the 7-point checklist). Without such a reference, the comparison between CNN and feature engineering describes relative method performance but cannot establish whether either approach is clinically useful or safe to deploy.
defender_raw:
This dermoscopy classification experiment is exceptionally well-designed for a controlled ML comparison. Filtering to high inter-rater agreement images (>90%) is not cherry-picking — it is standard practice in medical imaging benchmarks where label noise is a known confound. Evaluating on noisy labels would introduce a measurement problem that confounds model comparison; by filtering to high-agreement cases, the experiment can attribute performance differences to feature representation rather than label quality. A secondary sensitivity analysis on lower-confidence labels is explicitly included, demonstrating methodological rigor rather than concealment.

The stratified random split is the appropriate choice here. Critics who reflexively demand temporal splits misunderstand when temporal splits are warranted: they are necessary when deployment involves temporal generalization (e.g., predicting future patients who differ systematically from past patients). For per-image dermoscopy diagnosis, there is no meaningful temporal structure — a nevus photographed in year one is not systematically different from one photographed in year three. Imposing a temporal split would introduce unnecessary variance in class distribution without correcting for any genuine deployment concern.

The comparison is structurally fair. The baseline logistic regression receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search, identical class weighting, and access to a rich, domain-appropriate handcrafted feature set (~80-120 features from color, texture, and shape descriptors). This is not a weak baseline: it represents the traditional clinical image analysis pipeline. The choice to use macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric correctly prioritizes rare but clinically critical classes like melanoma — a detail that demonstrates genuine alignment between evaluation design and clinical stakes.

The preprocessing decision to fit normalization on the full dataset before splitting is a reasonable practical choice given the justification provided: it avoids instability from small training sets while the normalization parameters are applied uniformly. This is a minor methodological choice and any leakage effect is minimal given the nature of normalization statistics.
all_issues_raised: Normalization statistics computed from full dataset before splitting — direct train-test leakage for both CNN and traditional pipeline
Training restricted to high-agreement images creates survivorship bias overstating performance on real-world ambiguous cases
No clinical reference standard baseline (dermatologist accuracy or validated scoring rule) to contextualize CNN vs. feature comparison
Fraction excluded by agreement filter not reported; excluded-image characteristics not characterized
Leakage via feature scaling for traditional baseline also fit on entire dataset prior to splitting
all_issues_adjudicated: Normalization statistics computed from full dataset before splitting — direct train-test leakage for both CNN and traditional pipeline
No clinical reference standard baseline (dermatologist accuracy or validated scoring rule) to contextualize CNN vs. feature comparison
Fraction excluded by agreement filter not reported; excluded-image characteristics not characterized
Leakage via feature scaling for traditional baseline also fit on entire dataset prior to splitting
